content.problemStatement:
A combination is a selection of items from a set that has distinct items. 

If the number of items is $N$ and you have to select $K$ of them, then the number of ways is defined by the function $^NC_K$. 

$^NC_K$ is defined as $\frac{N!}{K! \cdot (N - K)!}$.

Why? we can arrange the $N$ items in $N!$ ways and then pick starting the $K$ elements of the bunch. \
But, since we do not care about he arrangement of first $K$ elements and the last $N - K$ elements, we remove the arrangements of these items.

For example, if you have $4$ fruits and you want to pick $2$ of the fruits, this can be done in $\frac{4!}{2! \cdot 2!}$ $=$ $6$ ways.

What if we want to select $K$ items from a set of $N$ distinct items and arrange them too, how many ways can we do this? 
- First pick $K$ elements from the bunch, this can be done in $^NC_K$ ways.
- Now, arrange the $K$ picked elements in $K!$ ways.
- Since these two are independent actions, use the rule of product and get, $\frac{N!}{ (N - K)!}$.

We define another function $^NP_K$ $=$ $\frac{N!}{ (N - K)!}$, the number of permutations of $K$ elements, selected from a bunch of $N$ elements.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 1000$
- $2 \leq N \leq 10^5$
- $1 \leq A_i \leq 10^9$
- The sum of $N$ across all test cases does not exceed $10^5$
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
- The first line of each test case contains a single integer $N$, denoting the length of array $A$.
- The second line of each test case contains $N$ space-separated integers $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$ — denoting the array $A$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the maximum value of $A_1+A_N$ you can get after several right rotations.
